title: Noncommutative geometry, smoothness, and Fukaya categories
speaker: Sheel Ganatra
abstract: Noncommutative geometry, as advocated by Konstevich, proposes to replace the study of (commutative) varieties by the study of their (noncommutative) dg/A-infinity categories of perfect complexes. Conveniently, these techniques can then also be applied to Fukaya categories. In this mini-course, we will review some basic properties and structures in noncommutative geometry, with an emphasis on the notion of "smoothness" of a category and its appearance in topology and both sides of homological mirror symmetry.

proof_stages.label: Hochschild invariants as noncommutative calculus
proof_stages.summary: The lecture recalls Hochschild homology and cohomology of dg/A∞ categories with bimodule coefficients, their algebraic structures, and the HKR philosophy that these invariants form a noncommutative analogue of calculus.

proof_stages.label: Shklyarov pairing from a proper bimodule
proof_stages.summary: For a proper bimodule viewed as a bilinear functor, functoriality of Hochschild homology together with Morita invariance, Künneth, and opposite-category invariance produces a bilinear pairing, whose diagonal specialization gives the Shklyarov pairing of a proper category and motivates the smooth-proper nondegeneracy statement.

proof_stages.label: Chern-character co-pairing and snake-relation proof
proof_stages.summary: A perfect bimodule gives a Hochschild co-pairing via its Chern character, and the snake relation between this co-pairing and a proper-bimodule pairing reduces, for the diagonal bimodule of a smooth proper category, to the identity map and hence proves finite-dimensionality and nondegeneracy.

proof_stages.label: Relative and twisted Shklyarov pairings
proof_stages.summary: The formalism is extended first to proper pairings between two different categories and then to a pairing between Hochschild homologies with coefficients in an invertible bimodule and its inverse, constructed by adding the needed bimodule morphism for functoriality with coefficients.

proof_stages.label: Open–closed maps and twisted Fukaya data
proof_stages.summary: For a compact symplectic manifold, the talk introduces the Fukaya category, open–closed and closed–open maps to quantum cohomology and Hochschild invariants, and for a symplectomorphism φ the graph bimodule and twisted open–closed map to fixed-point Floer cohomology.

proof_stages.label: Compatibility of open–closed maps with pairings
proof_stages.summary: A theorem states that ordinary and twisted open–closed maps intertwine Shklyarov-type pairings with the corresponding quantum or fixed-point Floer pairings, and therefore smooth subcategories yield injective open–closed maps by nondegeneracy.

proof_stages.label: Automatic generation from smoothness and rank
proof_stages.summary: Combining the injectivity result with Abouzaid’s generation criterion gives an automatic generation theorem: a smooth nonempty Fukaya subcategory satisfying the stated Hochschild-cohomology/quantum-cohomology rank condition split-generates, with consequent isomorphism statements for open–closed and closed–open maps.

examples.name: Commutative HKR and Mukai-integration model
examples.note: For perfect complexes on a complex variety, HKR identifies Hochschild invariants with forms and polyvector fields, and the proper-variety integration pairing motivates the categorical Shklyarov pairing.
examples.engagement_reason: The commutative HKR isomorphism and Mukai-style wedge-and-integrate pairing are described as the motivating model, but no specific variety or calculation is worked out.

examples.name: Wrapped versus compact Fukaya categories
examples.note: The wrapped Fukaya category and the compact Fukaya subcategory illustrate a proper pairing between two categories even when one category is not itself proper.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker specializes the general pairing to wrapped and compact Fukaya categories and checks that morphisms between noncompact and compact Lagrangians have finite-rank Floer homology, so the pairing lands in perfect complexes.

passes.reason: the wrapped and compact Fukaya categories illustrate a typical nonproper/proper pairing situation with finite Floer complexes

illustrates a useful proper Hom-pairing between wrapped and compact Fukaya categories rather than refuting a claim

examples.name: Coherent sheaves paired with compactly supported perfect complexes
examples.note: The usual Ext/Euler-type pairing between coherent sheaves and compactly supported perfect complexes is given as the algebraic-geometric analogue of the relative proper-pairing construction.

examples.name: Koszul duality for based-loop chains and cochains

examples.engagement_reason: Based-loop chains and cochains are given as the prototypical Koszul-dual pair, but the duality is only stated, not derived or computed.

examples.name: Line-bundle-twisted integration pairing
examples.note: Forms twisted by a line bundle and by its inverse provide the commutative pattern that leads directly to the definition of the bimodule-twisted Shklyarov pairing.
examples.engagement_reason: The line-bundle-twisted integration pairing is invoked as the commutative motivation for twisting, while the detailed construction is carried out abstractly for invertible bimodules rather than on a line-bundle example.

passes.reason: twisting forms by a line bundle and its inverse is a standard invertible-coefficient variant of integration pairing
